Name: heuristic_filter_before_llm
Title: Analyzing Rule-Based (Heuristic) Filters Prior to LLM Debate
Experiment: Develop a module to count sensitive system calls (e.g., connect, execve, openat) from syscalls.log before forwarding the context to the LLM. If the alert count is < 2, automatically label the package as Benign without triggering the costly LLM Debate phase.
expected_outcome: Drastically reduce API Token costs for clean packages (True Negatives). The False Positive Rate will drop sharply by avoiding LLM hallucinations, though there is a calculated risk of a slight decrease in the overall Detection Rate.

Name: neo4j_graph_representation
Title: Transforming Syscalls into Neo4j Graph Representations for LLM Token Optimization
Experiment: Immediately after Step 2 (Sandbox syscall extraction), implement a module to parse syscalls into a Graph format (Nodes: Process, File, IP; Edges: CONNECT, READ, WRITE) and ingest them into Neo4j. Instead of forcing the LLM to read lengthy raw logs, provide a condensed JSON graph structure or allow the LLM to autonomously execute Cypher queries (Graph RAG) to verify behaviors.
expected_outcome: Save 70-90% of Input Tokens by eliminating redundant text. Greatly improve the Detection Rate, as malicious Kill Chains (e.g., Process -> Open File -> Connect IP) become highly prominent within the Neo4j graph structure.

Name: few_shot_in_context_learning
Title: Investigating the Impact of Few-Shot In-Context Learning on Syscall Analysis
Experiment: Upgrade the analysis prompt in Step 4 (src/llm_analyzer.py) from Zero-Shot to Few-Shot. Inject 2 'Clean Syscall' examples (e.g., Ping check for version updates) and 2 'Malicious Syscall' examples (e.g., Opening a network Backdoor) as Ground Truth directly into the System Prompt before analyzing new logs. Measure the subsequent shifts in the Detection Rate.
expected_outcome: Prevent the LLM from being confused by unfamiliar OS logic. The Few-Shot reference examples provide a solid baseline, helping the AI generate more confident assessments and pushing the False Positive Rate down to extremely low levels.

Name: temporal_feature_enrichment
Title: Enriching LLM Context with Temporal Syscall Features for Improved Behavioral Analysis
Experiment: Modify the syscall processing pipeline (between Step 2 and Step 4) to extract simple temporal features from syscalls.log: (1) Parse timestamps to compute total execution duration, (2) Calculate syscall density (calls/second), (3) Identify burst patterns (count syscalls occurring within 100ms windows), (4) Measure time gaps between critical operations (first network call, first file write, first exec). Inject these metrics into the LLM analysis prompt in Step 4 as structured context (e.g., 'Temporal Analysis: 156 syscalls over 2.3s, density=67.8 calls/s, detected 3 burst clusters with >20 calls in 100ms'). Compare detection metrics (Accuracy, F1, FPR) against baseline to measure whether temporal context improves the LLM's behavioral assessment quality.

Name: syscall_argument_semantic_enrichment
Title: Semantic Enrichment of Syscall Arguments for Context-Aware Malware Detection
Experiment: Create `src/syscall_argument_parser.py` to process syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Extract and categorize syscall arguments into three semantic categories: (1) Network activity - parse IP addresses and domains from connect/sendto calls, flag external targets (non-localhost/non-private IPs), (2) File access - extract file paths from openat/open/read/write calls, flag sensitive system paths matching patterns /etc/*, /root/*, /sys/*, (3) Process execution - extract commands from execve calls, flag shell invocations (/bin/sh, /bin/bash) and script interpreters. Generate a concise 2-3 sentence summary (e.g., 'Network: 2 external connections detected. File: accessed /etc/passwd. Exec: invoked /bin/sh'). Prepend this summary to the existing syscall log context in the LLM prompt at Step 4. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR against baseline. Track token count increase to ensure overhead stays under 15% while improving detection quality through semantic context.

Name: statistical_baseline_anomaly_detection
Title: Statistical Baseline Anomaly Detection for Context-Aware Syscall Analysis
Experiment: Implement `src/statistical_profiler.py` with two phases: (1) Baseline computation - on first run, process all known-benign packages from the benchmark to compute median and 90th/95th percentile thresholds for key syscall frequencies (connect, execve, openat, write, sendto) and critical ratios (network_calls/total, exec_calls/total). Persist these baselines to a JSON file. (2) Anomaly detection - during evaluation of each test package, load the cached baselines and compute simple anomaly flags by comparing the package's syscall counts against percentile thresholds. Generate a concise summary (e.g., 'Anomaly Flags: connect count exceeds 95th percentile of benign packages (12 vs baseline 2), execve within normal range'). Inject this summary into the LLM prompt at Step 4 before the syscall log. If baselines don't exist, skip anomaly detection gracefully. Compare Accuracy, F1, FPR against baseline to measure whether statistical context improves detection quality and reduces false positives by providing comparative norms.

Name: two_tier_syscall_triage
Title: Two-Tier Syscall-Based Triage for Selective LLM Invocation
Experiment: Implement `src/syscall_triage.py` to create a two-tier filtering system between Step 2 and Step 4. Tier 1 (Fast Path): Parse syscalls.log and apply conservative rule-based filters - auto-label as Benign if the package meets ALL three criteria: (1) total syscall count <20, (2) zero network-related syscalls (connect, sendto, recvfrom, socket with AF_INET), (3) zero process execution syscalls (execve, execveat). Tier 2 (LLM Path): Forward all other packages to the existing LLM analysis pipeline in Step 4. Modify the evaluation pipeline to invoke syscall_triage immediately after syscall extraction. Track metrics: percentage of packages filtered at Tier 1, Tier 1 accuracy (false negatives), overall token savings, final Accuracy/F1/FPR. Compare against baseline to quantify token cost reduction while ensuring Detection Rate remains >95% and FPR stays minimal. The conservative Tier 1 rules are designed to only catch obviously benign packages with minimal system interaction.

Name: early_stage_syscall_triage
Title: Early-Stage Syscall Pattern Analysis for Progressive Risk Assessment
Experiment: Implement `src/early_stage_analyzer.py` that extracts and analyzes only the first 20 syscalls from syscalls.log. Define 4 concrete high-risk early-stage patterns: (1) any network syscall (connect, sendto, socket with AF_INET) in positions 1-10, (2) file access to sensitive paths (/etc/passwd, /root/*, /.ssh/*, /proc/*/mem) in positions 1-15, (3) execve syscall in positions 1-12, (4) ≥3 write syscalls to non-standard locations (outside /tmp, /home/user/.cache) in first 20 calls. Apply conservative decision rules: Fast Malicious (≥2 high-risk patterns detected → label Malicious, skip LLM), Fast Benign (first 20 syscalls contain ONLY openat/read/stat/access to standard library paths AND zero network/exec/sensitive-file syscalls → label Benign, skip LLM), Uncertain (all other cases → forward to Step 4 LLM). Integrate between Step 2 and Step 4. Measure: early-stage triage rate (% Fast Malicious, % Fast Benign, % Uncertain), per-path accuracy against ground truth, token cost reduction, overall Accuracy/F1/FPR vs baseline. Validate Detection Rate ≥95% and FPR remains minimal.

Name: mitre_attack_semantic_mapping
Title: MITRE ATT&CK Semantic Mapping for Knowledge-Driven Threat Assessment
Experiment: Implement `src/mitre_attack_mapper.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Create a minimal mapping of syscall patterns to high-risk MITRE ATT&CK techniques: (1) Network C2 - detect connect/sendto/socket syscalls → map to T1071 (Application Layer Protocol), (2) Code Execution - detect execve with shell/interpreter arguments → map to T1059 (Command Interpreter), (3) Sensitive File Access - detect reads/writes to /etc/passwd, /root/*, /.ssh/* → map to T1005 (Data from Local System), (4) Persistence - detect writes to autostart paths like /etc/rc*, ~/.bashrc, ~/.config/autostart → map to T1547 (Boot/Logon Autostart). Generate a concise summary listing detected technique IDs. Apply conservative filtering: if zero high-risk techniques detected AND total_syscalls<25, label as Fast Benign and skip LLM (save tokens). For all other packages, prepend ATT&CK summary to the LLM prompt in Step 4 (e.g., 'ATT&CK Techniques: T1071 (C2), T1059 (Execution)'). Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, percentage of packages filtered, token cost reduction, and validate that Detection Rate stays ≥95%. Compare against baseline to quantify improvements from structured threat intelligence context.

Name: syscall_dependency_chain_analysis
Title: Lightweight Syscall Dependency Chain Tracking for Multi-Stage Attack Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/dependency_chain_analyzer.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Use simple sequential parsing to track 3 critical dependency chains: (1) File-to-Network: detect any openat/read on a file followed by connect/sendto within the next 50 syscalls (indicates potential data exfiltration), (2) Network-to-Exec: detect recvfrom/read on network socket, followed by write to file, followed by execve on that file path within next 100 syscalls (indicates remote code execution), (3) Sensitive-to-Network: detect read from sensitive paths (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*) followed by any network syscall within next 30 syscalls (indicates credential theft). Track file descriptors minimally by parsing fd numbers from syscall arguments and return values. Generate a concise 1-2 sentence summary of detected chains (e.g., 'Dependency Chains: 1 File-to-Network pattern detected, 0 Network-to-Exec'). Apply conservative fast-path filtering: if zero chains detected AND total_syscalls<30 AND zero execve/connect syscalls, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise prepend chain summary to LLM prompt in Step 4. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate, percentage filtered, and token savings vs baseline.

Name: adaptive_semantic_compression
Title: Adaptive Semantic Compression with Selective Two-Stage Processing for Token Optimization
Experiment: Implement `src/adaptive_semantic_compressor.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Apply adaptive logic: (1) If syscall count ≤50, skip compression and pass raw log directly to Step 4 (compression overhead not justified), (2) If syscall count >50, invoke a lightweight compression pass using the same LLM model with a specialized 'Syscall Interpreter' prompt (temperature=0.3) to generate a 4-6 sentence behavioral summary organized by: initialization, file operations, network activity, process execution, and sensitive operations. (3) Pass both the compressed summary AND a truncated raw log (first 30 + last 20 syscalls) to the main analysis in Step 4, allowing the model to reference details if needed. Measure: percentage of packages compressed, average input token reduction for compressed packages, total token cost (compression + analysis) vs baseline, Accuracy/F1/FPR, and latency. Calculate break-even point where compression savings offset compression cost. Validate that detection quality is maintained while achieving net token reduction of ≥30% on packages with >50 syscalls.

Name: intra_call_multi_perspective_analysis
Title: Intra-Call Multi-Perspective Security Analysis for Single-Pass Threat Assessment
Experiment: Replace both Step 4 (llm_analyzer.py) and Step 6 (multi_agent_extraction.py) with a single-pass multi-perspective analysis. Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to send one structured API call requesting three parallel security perspectives: (1) Network Security Analyst - evaluates C2 communications and data exfiltration patterns, (2) System Integrity Analyst - evaluates file tampering and privilege escalation, (3) Behavioral Anomaly Analyst - evaluates execution patterns and obfuscation. Use Pydantic structured outputs to enforce each perspective returns: verdict (Benign/Malicious), confidence (0-100), reasoning (1 sentence). Implement simple weighted voting aggregation: calculate weighted_malicious_score = sum(confidence for perspectives voting Malicious) / sum(all confidence scores). If weighted_malicious_score > 0.5, final verdict is Malicious. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to bypass the multi-agent debate entirely when this mode is enabled. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate, total token cost (should be ~70% lower than baseline 3-agent debate), and latency (should be ~60% lower). Track perspective agreement rates to validate analytical diversity. Compare against baseline to demonstrate that single-pass multi-perspective analysis achieves comparable or better detection quality at significantly reduced cost and latency.

Name: motif_diversity_analysis
Title: Behavioral Motif Diversity Analysis for Enhanced Syscall Pattern Recognition
Experiment: Implement `src/motif_analyzer.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Extract all 3-grams of consecutive syscall types from the log (e.g., ['openat', 'read', 'close']). Compute three motif-based metrics: (1) Motif Diversity - ratio of unique 3-grams to total 3-grams (range 0-1, where low values suggest focused/repetitive behavior typical of malware), (2) High-Risk Motif Count - count 3-grams containing at least one critical syscall pair like connect+read, read+execve, openat+sendto, or any syscall accessing sensitive paths (/etc/passwd, /.ssh/*, /root/*), (3) Max Repetition Rate - find the most frequently occurring 3-gram and calculate its frequency as percentage of all 3-grams (high repetition suggests polling/beaconing behavior). Generate a concise summary: 'Motif Analysis: Diversity=0.34 (low), 5 high-risk motifs detected, max motif repetition=24%'. Apply conservative fast-path filtering: if diversity>0.75 AND high_risk_count=0 AND total_syscalls<25, label Fast Benign and skip LLM (saves tokens on obviously benign packages). Otherwise inject motif summary into Step 4 LLM prompt with brief context: 'Low diversity and high repetition often indicate automated malicious behavior.' Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate, percentage of packages filtered via fast-path, and token cost reduction vs baseline. Track correlation between motif metrics and ground truth labels to validate predictive value. This approach provides richer behavioral context than raw syscalls without cross-package dependencies or external data requirements.

Name: entropy_based_syscall_filtering
Title: Information-Theoretic Syscall Filtering for Noise Reduction and Signal Enhancement
Experiment: Implement `src/entropy_filter.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. For each syscall type in the log, extract the first argument (typically file path, file descriptor, or network address) from each occurrence. Compute Diversity Ratio = count of unique first arguments / total occurrences of that syscall type (range 0-1, where low values indicate highly repetitive low-information syscalls). Define a critical syscall whitelist that is NEVER filtered: connect, sendto, recvfrom, socket, execve, execveat, setuid, setgid, and any syscall where the first argument contains sensitive path patterns (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*, /proc/*/mem). Apply conservative filtering: remove syscalls where (syscall_type NOT IN whitelist AND Diversity_Ratio < 0.2 AND occurrence_count > 15). Implement a safety cap: if filtering would remove >50% of total syscalls, retain the syscalls with highest diversity ratios until the 50% threshold is met. Generate a summary: 'Entropy Filter: Removed 89/312 syscalls (28% reduction), diversity_ratio_threshold=0.2'. Pass the filtered log to Step 4. Measure: token reduction percentage (separately for benign vs malicious packages), Accuracy/F1/FPR vs baseline, Detection Rate (must be ≥95%), and percentage of packages hitting the 50% safety cap. Manually inspect 5 filtered logs to validate that removed syscalls are genuinely low-information.

Name: precedent_based_similarity_retrieval
Title: Precedent-Based Similarity Retrieval for Cross-Package Behavioral Learning
Experiment: Implement `src/behavioral_profiler.py` that extracts a compact 15-dimensional feature vector after Step 6 verdict: (1) Counts of 10 critical syscall types: connect, execve, sendto, socket, setuid, setgid, openat to sensitive paths (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*), write to sensitive paths (autostart locations), recvfrom, mmap, (2) 3 ratio features: network_calls/total_syscalls, exec_calls/total_syscalls, sensitive_file_ops/total_syscalls, (3) 2 temporal features: total_syscalls, syscall_density (calls/second, or 0 if duration unavailable). Normalize the vector to unit length immediately after extraction. Store profiles in an in-memory list with metadata: verdict, confidence, 1-sentence reasoning. Implement `src/similarity_matcher.py` that runs before Step 4: compute cosine similarity between current package profile and all cached profiles using normalized vectors. Retrieve precedents with similarity ≥0.5, up to top-3. Apply diversity constraint: if all retrieved precedents have the same verdict AND at least one opposite-verdict precedent exists with similarity ≥0.45, replace the lowest-similarity retrieved precedent with the highest-similarity opposite-verdict precedent. Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to inject precedent context only if precedents were found: 'Precedent Analysis: [1] similarity=0.78: Malicious (conf=92%, network backdoor), [2] similarity=0.71: Benign (conf=89%, HTTP client), [3] similarity=0.68: Malicious (conf=85%, credential theft).' Prompt emphasizes: 'Use precedents as reference points but assess independently based on current syscall evidence.' Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR vs baseline, precedent consistency rate (% where top-precedent verdict matches final verdict), average similarity of retrieved precedents, token overhead. Gracefully skip precedent injection for first package or when no precedents meet threshold.

Name: argument_level_dataflow_tracking
Title: Argument-Level Data Flow Tracking for Semantic Malware Behavior Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/dataflow_tracker.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4 using simple string parsing with graceful error handling. For each syscall line, extract: (1) syscall name, (2) first argument (typically fd or file path), (3) return value (for syscalls that create fds like openat). Build two simple lookup tables: fd_to_source (maps fd numbers to their origin: 'network_socket' for fds from socket/connect, 'sensitive_file' for fds from opening /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*, 'standard_file' for other files, 'unknown' as fallback) and path_operations (tracks which file paths were read/written/executed). Detect 4 unambiguous critical flow patterns using direct argument matching: (1) Sensitive-to-Network: any fd created by opening sensitive paths is later used in sendto/write where the destination fd is a network socket, (2) Network-to-Exec: a file path is written by a network socket fd, then that same path appears in execve, (3) Network-to-Sensitive: a network socket fd writes to sensitive paths like ~/.ssh/*, /etc/*, (4) Read-then-Send: the same fd is used in both read and sendto/write-to-network within 30 syscalls. For each detected flow, record the pattern type and the specific fds/paths involved. Generate concise summary: 'Data Flow: 1 Sensitive-to-Network flow [fd=3: /etc/passwd → sendto], 0 Network-to-Exec'. Apply conservative fast-path: if zero flows AND zero connect/execve syscalls AND total_syscalls<25, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise inject flow summary into Step 4 prompt. If parsing fails on any line, skip that line and continue. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate (≥95%), fast-path filtering rate, token savings. Manually inspect 5 detected flows to validate parsing correctness.

Name: error_pattern_consistency_hints
Title: Error Pattern Consistency Hints: Reducing Verdict Inconsistency Through Minimal Mistake Context
Experiment: Implement `src/error_pattern_tracker.py` that maintains an in-memory error database during benchmark evaluation. After Step 6 verdict in `evaluate_shield.py`, compare verdict against ground truth. For misclassifications, extract a minimal error signature: (1) error type (FP/FN), (2) set of all 3-gram syscall sequences from the package (e.g., {'openat-read-close', 'connect-sendto-close'}), (3) one-sentence discriminator extracted from the LLM's reasoning (first complete sentence mentioning a specific syscall or behavior). Store in error database. Before Step 4, implement simple matching in `src/error_matcher.py`: extract 3-grams from current package, compute overlap with each error signature (count of shared 3-grams), retrieve the single most similar error if overlap ≥3 3-grams. Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to inject a concise hint only when match found: 'Consistency Check: A previous similar package (shared syscall patterns: [list 3 shared 3-grams]) was a [FALSE POSITIVE / FALSE NEGATIVE]. Key discriminator: [extracted sentence]. Verify this aspect carefully to avoid repeating the error.' Measure primary metrics: Accuracy, F1, FPR vs baseline. Measure consistency metrics: (1) repeat error rate (% of errors on packages with similar 3-gram patterns to previous errors), (2) verdict consistency (% of similar packages receiving same verdict), (3) temporal improvement (compare error rate in first half vs second half of benchmark). Track token overhead (should be <5% due to single-hint injection). Gracefully skip hint injection for packages with no matches or during first 10% of benchmark. The goal is demonstrating that minimal error context improves consistency, not gaming the benchmark.

Name: reasoning_quality_debate_routing
Title: Reasoning Quality-Aware Debate Routing for Improved Verdict Reliability
Experiment: Implement `src/reasoning_quality_scorer.py` that evaluates LLM reasoning after Step 4 using 3 lightweight rule-based checks: (1) Specificity Score (0-40 points) - count explicit syscall name mentions (openat, connect, execve, etc.) and award 2 points per mention up to 20 points, plus award 1 point per line number reference up to 20 points, (2) Hedge Penalty (0-30 points) - count hedge words ('might', 'possibly', 'perhaps', 'unclear', 'ambiguous', 'seems', 'appears') and subtract 5 points per occurrence from base 30, floor at 0, (3) Contradiction Detection (0-30 points) - use simple regex to detect opposing sentiment phrases within the same reasoning (patterns like 'benign.*malicious|malicious.*benign' or 'legitimate.*suspicious|suspicious.*legitimate' within 100 characters), subtract 15 points per contradiction from base 30. Sum to get quality_score (0-100). Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to compute quality score after initial analysis and extract both verdict and confidence. Implement `src/quality_aware_router.py` that applies adaptive debate routing based on combined quality and confidence signals: (1) Low Quality (score <50) - always trigger full 3-agent debate regardless of confidence, as suspect reasoning requires validation, (2) High Quality (score ≥70) AND High Confidence (≥85%) - skip debate entirely (fast-path), (3) High Quality (score ≥70) AND Medium Confidence (70-84%) - use 2-agent debate, (4) Medium Quality (50-69) - use standard confidence-based routing: skip debate if confidence ≥90%, else 3-agent debate, (5) All other cases - full 3-agent debate. Modify `src/multi_agent_extraction.py` to accept agent count parameter (0, 2, or 3). Update `evaluate_shield.py` to route through quality_aware_router before debate phase. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate vs baseline, token cost (expect 25-35% reduction from fast-path on high-quality high-confidence cases), distribution across routing tiers, correlation between quality score and verdict correctness (validate that low-quality reasoning predicts errors), false positive rate stratified by quality tier (expect FPs concentrated in low-quality tier). Track percentage of low-quality cases that flip verdict after debate to validate intervention effectiveness.

Name: adversarial_evasion_pattern_detection
Title: Adversarial Evasion Pattern Detection for Robust Malware Identification
Experiment: Implement `src/evasion_detector.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4 to detect 3 high-confidence anti-analysis evasion patterns: (1) VM/Sandbox Detection - detect reads from known VM detection paths: /sys/class/dmi/id/product_name, /sys/devices/virtual/dmi, /proc/scsi/scsi, /proc/cpuinfo (these paths are rarely accessed by legitimate packages), (2) Indirect Code Execution - detect mmap syscalls with PROT_EXEC flag (check for 'PROT_EXEC' in syscall arguments) that are NOT followed by any execve/execveat within the next 50 syscalls (suggests in-memory code execution rather than normal program launching), (3) Shell Redirection Obfuscation - detect >=3 pipe/pipe2/dup/dup2 syscalls combined with execve that invokes shell interpreters (check for '/bin/sh' or '/bin/bash' in execve arguments), indicating complex command chaining typical of obfuscated malware. For each pattern, record binary flag (0/1) and evidence line numbers. Compute evasion_score = sum of flags (0-3). Generate concise summary: 'Evasion Analysis: score=2/3, VM Detection (read /proc/cpuinfo), Indirect Execution (mmap PROT_EXEC at line 45, no execve follows)'. Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to inject evasion summary into Step 4 prompt: 'Anti-analysis evasion patterns detected: [summary]. Evasion techniques are strong indicators of malicious intent.' Implement routing logic: if evasion_score>=2 AND package contains at least one capability syscall (connect, sendto, execve, setuid), override confidence-based routing to ensure minimum 2-agent debate (use 2 agents if confidence would skip debate, keep 3 agents if already routing to full debate). Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate vs baseline. Separately analyze detection improvement on packages with evasion_score>=1 to validate pattern effectiveness. Track false positive rate on benign packages to ensure evasion flags don't over-trigger. Expect <5% token overhead with improved recall on sophisticated evasive malware.

Name: two_stage_adversarial_reasoning
Title: Two-Stage Adversarial Reasoning for False Positive Reduction Through Forced Alternative Explanations
Experiment: Implement `src/adversarial_analyzer.py` that replaces Step 4 and Step 6 with a two-stage sequential process. Stage 1 (Threat Identification): Analyze syscalls.log using a 'Security Analyst' prompt focused on identifying suspicious patterns. Use structured output to extract {threats: list[str], evidence_summary: str (max 3 sentences), preliminary_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', confidence: int}. Keep threats as simple strings (e.g., 'Network connection to external IP after reading /etc/passwd') rather than complex nested structures. Stage 2 (Adversarial Validation): Send a 'Defense Analyst' prompt that receives both the full syscalls.log AND the Stage 1 output (threats list, evidence summary, preliminary verdict). Prompt emphasizes: 'For each identified threat, determine if there is a legitimate benign explanation by examining the full syscall context. If all threats can be explained as normal behavior, override to Benign. If any threat remains unexplained, confirm Malicious.' Use structured output: {benign_explanations: list[str], unexplained_threats: list[str], final_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', confidence: int, reasoning: str (max 4 sentences)}. The key innovation: Stage 2 must explicitly categorize each threat as either explained or unexplained, forcing engagement with prosecutor's evidence rather than providing independent analysis. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to invoke adversarial_analyzer instead of separate llm_analyzer and multi_agent_extraction. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR (primary target: expect 30-50% FPR reduction from forced alternative explanations), Detection Rate (must maintain ≥95%), total token cost (expect ~33% reduction vs baseline 3-agent debate since we use 2 sequential calls instead of 3 parallel agents), reasoning quality (manually review 10 false positive cases from baseline to verify if adversarial validation would have caught them). Track percentage of cases where Stage 2 overrides Stage 1 verdict to Benign (validates defense mechanism effectiveness) and percentage where Stage 2 confirms Malicious with strengthened reasoning.

Name: temporal_phase_transition_detection
Title: Temporal Phase Transition Detection for Time-Delayed Malware Identification
Experiment: Implement `src/phase_transition_detector.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Skip if total_syscalls<30 (need minimum 2 overlapping windows). Use sliding windows of size 15 syscalls with stride 10 (60% overlap for smoother transition tracking). For each window, compute a 6-dimensional feature vector: normalized counts (count/15) of {connect, execve, sendto, setuid, reads_from_sensitive_paths (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*), writes_to_sensitive_paths (autostart locations)}. Compute cosine distance between consecutive window vectors. Calculate two metrics: (1) max_divergence = maximum distance across all window transitions, (2) divergence_std = standard deviation of all distances. Generate summary: 'Phase Analysis: max_divergence=0.73 at window transition 3→4, divergence_std=0.28 (high variance suggests erratic behavior)'. Apply fast-path filtering: if max_divergence<0.25 AND divergence_std<0.15 AND zero {connect,execve,sendto,setuid} AND total_syscalls<35, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise inject phase summary into Step 4 LLM prompt with interpretation: 'High max_divergence (>0.65) indicates staged activation. High divergence_std (>0.30) indicates erratic/obfuscated behavior. Both are malware indicators.' Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate vs baseline. Track correlation between divergence metrics and ground truth. Separately analyze detection rate on malicious packages with max_divergence>0.60 to validate staged malware detection.

Name: forced_alternative_explanation_analysis
Title: Forced Alternative Explanation Analysis for Single-Pass Metacognitive Reasoning
Experiment: Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to replace Step 4 with a structured single-pass analysis that forces consideration of alternative explanations. Use a prompt that explicitly instructs: 'Identify 2-3 ambiguous or suspicious behaviors from the syscalls. For each behavior, provide both a plausible benign explanation AND a plausible malicious explanation. Then weigh all evidence to determine your final verdict.' Use Pydantic structured outputs to enforce the format: {ambiguous_behaviors: list[{behavior: str (1 sentence), benign_explanation: str (1 sentence), malicious_explanation: str (1 sentence)}], final_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', confidence: int, reasoning: str (max 3 sentences)}. The structured format forces the LLM to explicitly consider both interpretations before committing to a verdict, reducing overconfident false positives. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to use this new analyzer and skip the multi-agent debate (Step 6) entirely when this mode is enabled. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 30-45% reduction from forced alternative consideration), Detection Rate (maintain ≥95%), token cost (expect 40-50% reduction vs baseline 3-agent debate since we use 1 call instead of 3), reasoning quality (manually review 10 false positive cases from baseline to verify if forced alternative explanations would have prevented the error). Track the percentage of cases where the LLM identifies ambiguous behaviors and whether the final verdict aligns with the stronger explanation type.

Name: objective_consistency_verification
Title: Objective Multi-Perspective Consistency Verification for Adaptive Debate Routing
Experiment: Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to replace Step 4 with structured single-pass multi-perspective analysis. Use Pydantic structured outputs: {perspectives: [{role: str, verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', confidence: int, reasoning: str (max 2 sentences)}] (exactly 3 perspectives: 'Network Security', 'System Integrity', 'Behavioral Analysis'), final_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', final_confidence: int, final_reasoning: str (max 2 sentences)}. Prompt instructs: 'Analyze syscalls from three independent security perspectives. Each perspective assesses independently with its own verdict, confidence, and reasoning. Then provide your final integrated assessment.' Implement `src/objective_consistency_scorer.py` that computes consistency from structured output using two objective metrics: (1) Verdict Agreement Score (0-70 points): 70 if all 3 perspectives match final_verdict, 50 if 2/3 match, 0 if perspectives split or majority disagrees with final, (2) Confidence Strength Score (0-30 points): when ≥2 perspectives agree on verdict, compute mean confidence of agreeing perspectives - award 30 if mean≥85, 20 if mean≥75, 10 if mean≥65, 0 otherwise. Sum to get objective_consistency_score (0-100). Implement routing in `src/consistency_router.py`: (1) High Consistency (score≥80): accept final_verdict, skip debate (0 agents), (2) Medium Consistency (60-79): lightweight 2-agent debate, (3) Low Consistency (<60): full 3-agent debate. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to compute consistency score and route before Step 6. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR vs baseline, token cost (target 40-55% reduction), correlation between objective_consistency_score and verdict correctness, distribution across consistency tiers, per-tier accuracy to validate high-consistency reliability, percentage of low-consistency cases where debate changes verdict.

Name: entropy_based_contrastive_routing
Title: Entropy-Based Contrastive Routing: Using Token-Level Uncertainty for Adaptive Dual-Perspective Analysis
Experiment: Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to capture token log probabilities during Step 4 initial analysis by adding API parameters to request logprobs (e.g., `logprobs=True` for OpenAI, `top_k_logprobs=5` for compatible APIs). For each token in the generated reasoning, compute Shannon entropy = -sum(p_i * log2(p_i)) over the returned probability distribution. Calculate mean_entropy across all reasoning tokens as a global uncertainty metric. Implement two-tier routing based on entropy threshold: (1) Low Uncertainty (mean_entropy < 1.0): Model is confident in its reasoning → accept initial verdict and confidence, skip additional analysis, (2) High Uncertainty (mean_entropy >= 1.0): Model shows significant uncertainty → trigger contrastive follow-up analysis. For high-uncertainty cases, send a structured contrastive prompt: 'Your initial analysis showed uncertainty. Provide: (1) Strongest evidence and reasoning for Malicious verdict (max 2 sentences), (2) Strongest evidence and reasoning for Benign verdict (max 2 sentences), (3) Final determination weighing both perspectives.' Use Pydantic structured output: {malicious_case: str, benign_case: str, final_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', final_confidence: int, reasoning: str (max 2 sentences)}. The contrastive format forces explicit consideration of alternative explanations when the model is genuinely uncertain. Skip multi-agent debate (Step 6) when this mode is enabled. Handle API compatibility gracefully: if logprobs unavailable, fall back to standard analysis without entropy routing. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR vs baseline (expect 20-30% FPR reduction from catching uncertain false positives), Detection Rate (maintain >=95%), token cost (expect 10-20% overhead from contrastive analysis on uncertain cases only), percentage of cases triggering contrastive analysis (expect 25-40%), correlation between mean_entropy and verdict correctness (validate that high entropy predicts errors), improvement rate on high-entropy cases (percentage where contrastive analysis changes verdict and improves correctness). Compare entropy-based routing against confidence-based routing to demonstrate that generation uncertainty is a better signal than self-reported confidence.

Name: causal_trace_extraction
Title: Causal Trace Extraction: Semantic Compression via Security-Relevant Syscall Identification
Experiment: Implement `src/causal_trace_extractor.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Apply extraction only if total_syscalls >= 50 (skip overhead for small logs). Use a lightweight LLM call (temperature=0.2) with structured output prompt: 'Analyze this syscall log to identify security-relevant syscalls. Return: (1) syscall_types: list of syscall names that are security-relevant (e.g., connect, execve, sendto), (2) path_patterns: list of file path substrings indicating sensitive access (e.g., /etc/passwd, /.ssh/, /root/), (3) reasoning: brief explanation (max 2 sentences).' Use Pydantic structured output: {syscall_types: list[str], path_patterns: list[str], reasoning: str}. Extract syscalls using simple matching: keep any syscall line where (syscall name in syscall_types) OR (any path_pattern substring appears in the line). Apply mandatory safety: always retain syscalls containing critical keywords {connect, execve, sendto, recvfrom, socket, setuid, setgid, /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/, /root/, /proc/*/mem} even if not in extraction output. If final causal trace retains >75% of syscalls, pass full log instead (extraction not worthwhile). Otherwise pass causal trace to Step 4 with prefix: 'Causal Trace: X/Y syscalls retained.' Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to handle both formats. Measure: percentage of packages where extraction applied, average retention rate, input token reduction for Step 4 (target 50-70% on extracted packages), extraction overhead tokens, net total token cost vs baseline (target 35-50% reduction overall), Accuracy, F1, FPR, Detection Rate (must be >=95%). Manually inspect 5 causal traces to validate correctness. The structured output ensures reliable parsing and the safety validation prevents critical information loss.

Name: semantic_consistency_validation
Title: Cross-Syscall Semantic Consistency Validation for Context-Aware Anomaly Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/semantic_consistency_validator.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Use simple regex to extract: file paths from openat/read/write/execve, IP/domains from connect/sendto, commands from execve arguments. Track: files_written (set of paths with line numbers), files_executed (set of paths with line numbers), sensitive_reads (paths matching /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/* with line numbers), network_ops (line numbers), privilege_ops (setuid/setgid line numbers). Apply 4 semantic consistency rules: (1) Write-Execute Chain - detect file written to temp locations (/tmp/, /var/tmp/, .cache/, downloads/) then executed within 50 syscalls, (2) Credential-Network Coupling - sensitive file read followed by network syscall within 30 syscalls, (3) Privilege-Path Mismatch - setuid/setgid followed within 20 syscalls by execve where path NOT in (/bin/, /sbin/, /usr/bin/, /usr/sbin/), (4) Shell Obfuscation - execve of /bin/sh or /bin/bash where arguments contain suspicious substrings (base64, pipe after curl/wget, eval, exec with command substitution). For each detected rule violation, record flag type and evidence line numbers. Generate summary: 'Semantic Flags: [count] detected [flag_types with line ranges]'. Apply fast-path: if zero flags AND zero critical syscalls (connect/execve/sendto/setuid) AND total_syscalls<25, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise inject flag summary into Step 4 LLM prompt with context about semantic inconsistencies. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR (expect 20-30% reduction from catching semantic anomalies), Detection Rate (expect 3-5% improvement on obfuscated malware), token cost (expect 20-30% reduction from fast-path filtering), distribution of flags across benign vs malicious packages.

Name: online_behavioral_clustering
Title: Online Behavioral Clustering for Adaptive Benign Pattern Recognition
Experiment: Implement `src/online_cluster_manager.py` that maintains dynamic benign behavioral clusters during evaluation. Extract a 12-dimensional feature vector after Step 2: normalized counts (count/total_syscalls) of 12 critical syscall types: connect, execve, sendto, socket, setuid, setgid, openat, read, write, mmap, recvfrom, close. Handle edge case: if total_syscalls=0, set all features to 0.0 and skip clustering for that package. Initialize with empty benign cluster list. For the first 15 packages, skip clustering and use standard pipeline to build foundation. Starting from package 16: compute feature vector, find nearest benign cluster using Euclidean distance. Apply conservative 2-tier routing: (1) Fast Benign Path (distance to nearest benign cluster ≤0.30 AND cluster has ≥5 members AND cluster purity ≥90% benign AND package has NO suspicious combinations: no connect+sendto within 50 syscalls, no setuid+execve within 20 syscalls, no reads from /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow or /.ssh/* or /root/*): accept Benign verdict immediately, skip LLM entirely, (2) Standard Analysis (all other cases): proceed with normal Step 4 and Step 6 pipeline. After Step 6 verdict, if verdict is Benign AND confidence ≥85%, update clusters: if package is within 0.35 distance of existing benign cluster, add to cluster and update centroid using incremental mean (new_centroid = (old_centroid * old_count + new_vector) / (old_count + 1), increment member count). Otherwise create new singleton benign cluster. Track cluster metadata: centroid vector (12-dim), member count, purity (benign_count/total_count). Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to initialize cluster manager before evaluation loop and route each package through cluster manager before Step 4. Measure: Accuracy (must match baseline), F1, FPR (must match or improve vs baseline), Detection Rate (must be ≥95%), token cost (target 20-30% reduction from Fast Benign routing), percentage of packages routed via Fast Benign path (expect 15-20% of total), cluster statistics (number of benign clusters formed, average cluster size, average purity), false negative rate on Fast Benign path (must be <1% to validate safety). The conservative multi-constraint approach ensures token savings without sacrificing detection quality.

Name: syscall_interleaving_rhythm_detection
Title: Syscall Category Interleaving Detection for Temporal Anomaly Identification
Experiment: Implement `src/interleaving_detector.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Skip if total_syscalls<15. Map each syscall to 3 categories: IO (openat, read, write, close, stat, access, lseek), NET (connect, sendto, recvfrom, socket, bind, listen, accept), EXEC (execve, execveat, setuid, setgid, mmap with PROT_EXEC). Syscalls not matching these categories are mapped to OTHER and included in interleaving calculations. Compute global interleaving_rate = count of category transitions / (total_syscalls - 1), range 0-1 where high values indicate frequent context switching between operation types. Use sliding windows of size 10 with stride 5 to compute local interleaving rates for each window. Calculate interleaving_variance = std_dev(local_interleaving_rates across all windows). Generate summary: 'Interleaving Analysis: global_rate=0.68 (high switching), variance=0.22 (erratic rhythm)'. Apply two-tier interpretation: (1) Focused Execution (global_rate<0.35 AND variance<0.15): operations are batched by type, typical of benign software, (2) Erratic Execution (global_rate>0.60 OR variance>0.25): frequent context switching suggests evasive or malicious behavior. Apply conservative fast-path filtering: if global_rate<0.30 AND variance<0.12 AND zero {connect,execve,sendto,setuid,setgid} AND total_syscalls<30, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise inject interleaving summary into Step 4 LLM prompt with interpretation guidance. Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 20-30% reduction from catching rhythmic false positives), Detection Rate (>=95%), token cost (target 15-25% reduction from fast-path), percentage of packages filtered, correlation between interleaving metrics and ground truth labels, separately analyze detection rate on malicious packages with global_rate>0.65 to validate that high interleaving correlates with malicious behavior.

Name: entity_interaction_graph_topology
Title: Lightweight Entity Interaction Pattern Analysis for Multi-Stage Attack Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/entity_interaction_analyzer.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Use simple regex parsing with graceful error handling to extract: (1) Sensitive files - paths matching {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*} from any syscall, (2) Network endpoints - IP addresses or domains from connect/sendto (extract using basic pattern matching, skip if parsing fails), (3) Executed commands - paths from execve arguments. Track three entity sets and compute 4 simple metrics: (1) Sensitive Resource Count - size of unique sensitive file set, measures credential access breadth, (2) Network Endpoint Count - size of unique network endpoint set, measures C2/exfiltration infrastructure, (3) Sensitive-Network Proximity - minimum syscall distance between any sensitive file access and any network operation (scan linearly, track line numbers), measures exfiltration directness, (4) Execution Diversity - count of unique executed paths, measures lateral movement. Generate summary: 'Entity Interaction: 2 sensitive resources, 3 network endpoints, proximity=12 syscalls, 1 execution'. Apply conservative fast-path: if sensitive_count=0 AND network_count=0 AND execution_diversity<=1 AND total_syscalls<30, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise inject summary into Step 4 LLM prompt: 'Multiple sensitive resources (>1) combined with multiple network endpoints (>1) and low proximity (<30 syscalls) indicate coordinated exfiltration. High execution diversity (>2) suggests lateral movement.' Measure Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 20-30% reduction), Detection Rate (target 5-8% improvement on multi-stage attacks with multiple sensitive resources or network endpoints), token cost (target 20-30% reduction from fast-path), percentage filtered. Separately analyze detection rate on packages with sensitive_count>=2 AND network_count>=2 to validate multi-target attack detection. Implementation uses only basic Python data structures (sets, lists) with no external dependencies.

Name: intent_driven_hypothesis_matching
Title: Intent-Driven Hypothesis Matching: Semantic Attack Pattern Recognition Through Hardcoded Intent Signatures
Experiment: Implement `src/intent_matcher.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Define a minimal hardcoded intent library with 5 attack intents, each containing: (1) intent name, (2) critical syscall pairs (2-element tuples of syscall types that must appear in order within 50 syscalls), (3) argument constraints (semantic requirements like 'network destination must be external IP' or 'file path must match sensitive patterns'), (4) risk weight. The 5 intents: Credential Exfiltration (pairs: [('openat', 'connect'), ('read', 'sendto')], constraints: first syscall accesses {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*} AND second syscall targets external IP, weight=0.9), Remote Code Execution (pairs: [('recvfrom', 'write'), ('write', 'execve')], constraints: write and execve operate on same file path, weight=0.95), Backdoor Installation (pairs: [('socket', 'bind'), ('write', 'execve')], constraints: write targets autostart paths {~/.bashrc, /etc/rc*, ~/.config/autostart/*}, weight=0.85), Privilege Escalation (pairs: [('setuid', 'execve'), ('setgid', 'execve')], constraints: execve path NOT in standard system directories {/bin/*, /sbin/*, /usr/bin/*, /usr/sbin/*}, weight=0.9), Obfuscated Execution (pairs: [('mmap', 'read'), ('execve', 'pipe')], constraints: mmap has PROT_EXEC flag OR execve invokes shell {/bin/sh, /bin/bash} with pipe syscalls nearby, weight=0.8). Use simple regex parsing with graceful error handling to extract: file paths, network destinations (classify as external if not 127.0.0.1/localhost/10.*/192.168.*/172.16-31.*), executed commands, file descriptors for tracking. For each intent, compute argument-aware match score: (1) Satisfied Pairs = count of critical pairs where both syscalls present in correct order within 50 syscalls AND argument constraints satisfied, (2) Pair Coverage = satisfied_pairs / total_pairs_in_signature (range 0-1), (3) Final intent_score = pair_coverage * risk_weight. Find top matching intent if score >= 0.4. Apply conservative three-tier routing: (1) High Intent Match (score >= 0.7): Inject focused validation prompt into Step 4: 'Strong match to [intent_name] attack pattern detected (score=X). Critical evidence: [list satisfied pairs with line numbers]. Verify: Is this genuine [intent_name] or legitimate behavior with similar syscall patterns?' Use structured output to force explicit validation, (2) Medium Intent Match (0.4 <= score < 0.7): Inject lightweight context: 'Partial match to [intent_name] (score=X). Consider whether syscalls align with this attack intent.', (3) No Intent Match (score < 0.4 for all intents): Apply fast-path filtering if zero critical syscalls (connect, execve, sendto, setuid) AND total_syscalls<25, label Fast Benign and skip LLM. Otherwise standard analysis. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to route through intent_matcher before Step 4. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 25-35% reduction from intent-grounded validation catching false positives), Detection Rate (target 5-8% improvement from argument-aware matching), token cost (target 20-30% reduction from fast-path + focused validation prompts), distribution across routing tiers, correlation between intent_score and ground truth, manual review of 10 High Intent Match cases to validate that focused prompts improve verdict quality.

Name: structural_complexity_routing
Title: Structural Complexity-Based Routing: Two-Tier Analysis Depth Selection via Syscall Diversity Metrics
Experiment: Implement `src/structural_complexity_scorer.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Compute two simple metrics: (1) Type Diversity Score (0-50 points) - calculate unique_syscall_types / total_syscalls (range 0-1), normalize to 0-50 scale by multiplying by 50, measures behavioral variety, (2) Critical Density Score (0-50 points) - count syscalls in critical set {connect, execve, sendto, recvfrom, socket, setuid, setgid, mmap with PROT_EXEC, reads/writes to sensitive paths /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*}, compute critical_count / total_syscalls (range 0-1), normalize to 0-50 scale, measures security-relevant operation concentration. Sum to get structural_complexity (0-100). Implement `src/complexity_router.py` with binary routing: Low Complexity Path (score < 35): Apply conservative rule-based classification - if critical_count = 0, label Fast Benign and skip LLM entirely. If critical_count > 0 but score still < 35 (indicates repetitive critical operations like polling), pass to Step 4 with compressed context containing only critical syscalls plus 10 surrounding syscalls for each critical operation, skip Step 6 debate. High Complexity Path (score >= 35): Route to full baseline pipeline (Step 4 + Step 6) as these packages exhibit diverse behavior requiring deep reasoning. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to compute structural_complexity and route accordingly. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR vs baseline (expect maintained or slight improvement from appropriate routing), Detection Rate (must be >=95%), token cost (target 35-45% reduction from Low Complexity fast-path and compression), distribution between paths (expect 30-40% Low Complexity, 60-70% High Complexity), per-path accuracy to validate routing decisions, correlation between structural_complexity and verdict correctness (validate that low-complexity packages are genuinely easier to classify). The core hypothesis: structurally simple packages (low diversity, low critical density) can be handled efficiently with rules or compressed analysis, while structurally complex packages (high diversity or high critical density) require full LLM reasoning to understand nuanced behavioral interactions.

Name: argument_taint_flow_filtering
Title: Argument Taint Flow Filtering: Lightweight FD-Level Taint Tracking for Semantic Malware Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/argument_taint_tracker.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Use simple regex parsing with graceful error handling to track two fd categories: (1) Sensitive FDs - extract fd numbers from return values of openat/open syscalls where the file path argument matches sensitive patterns {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*}, store in sensitive_fds set with line numbers, (2) Network FDs - extract fd numbers from return values of socket/connect syscalls, store in network_fds set with line numbers. Detect 2 unambiguous taint violations by scanning subsequent syscalls: (1) Sensitive-to-Network - any fd in sensitive_fds appears as argument in sendto/write syscalls where the destination fd is in network_fds (indicates credential exfiltration), record violation type and line numbers, (2) Network-to-Exec - any fd in network_fds is used in write syscall to a file path, then that exact file path appears in execve within next 100 syscalls (indicates remote code execution), record violation type and line numbers. If fd parsing fails on any line, skip that line and continue. Generate concise summary: 'Taint Flow: [count] violations - [list types with evidence line numbers]'. Apply conservative fast-path filtering: if zero violations detected AND zero fds in sensitive_fds AND zero fds in network_fds AND total_syscalls<25, label Fast Benign and skip LLM (package has no sensitive file access or network activity). Otherwise inject taint summary into Step 4 LLM prompt: 'FD Taint Analysis: [summary]. Violations indicate argument-level dataflow between security-critical resources.' Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to route through taint tracker before Step 4. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 20-30% reduction from providing argument-level context that prevents LLM misinterpretation), Detection Rate (target 3-5% improvement on multi-stage attacks with fd reuse), token cost (target 25-35% reduction from fast-path filtering on packages with no sensitive/network fds), percentage filtered via fast-path, correlation between violation detection and ground truth malicious labels, manual inspection of 5 detected violations to validate correctness.

Name: bigram_perplexity_context_enrichment
Title: Bigram Perplexity Context Enrichment: Probabilistic Syscall Transition Analysis for Enhanced LLM Reasoning
Experiment: Implement `src/bigram_perplexity_analyzer.py` with two-phase operation: (1) Training Phase - at evaluation start in `evaluate_shield.py`, use first 70% of benign packages as training set (skip training if <10 benign packages available). For each training package, extract ordered syscall type sequence from syscalls.log. Build bigram transition matrix: count all syscall[i]→syscall[i+1] transitions. Keep top 40 most frequent syscall types, map others to 'OTHER'. Apply Laplace smoothing (add-1 to all cells) and normalize rows to get P(next|current). Compute median perplexity across training set: for each training package, calculate perplexity = 2^(-sum(log2(P(syscall[i+1]|syscall[i]))) / (N-1)), store median as baseline_perplexity. (2) Evaluation Phase - for each test package, compute perplexity using trained model. Skip if sequence_length<5. Calculate perplexity_ratio = package_perplexity / baseline_perplexity (ratio>1 means more surprising than typical benign). Identify top 3 most surprising transitions (lowest P(next|current) values) with their probabilities and line numbers. Implement two-tier routing in `src/perplexity_router.py`: (1) Fast Benign Path (perplexity_ratio < 0.8 AND zero critical syscalls {connect,execve,sendto,setuid,setgid} AND total_syscalls<30): sequence follows normal patterns very closely → label Benign, skip LLM, save tokens, (2) LLM Analysis with Perplexity Context (all other cases): inject concise summary into Step 4 prompt: 'Perplexity Analysis: ratio=[X] (package is [X]x more/less surprising than typical benign behavior). Most anomalous transitions: [list top 3 with probabilities and line numbers]. High ratios (>2.0) suggest unusual execution patterns.' Proceed with standard Step 4 and Step 6. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to train model before evaluation loop (gracefully skip training if insufficient data) and route through perplexity_router before Step 4. Measure on test set (remaining 30% benign + all malicious): Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 15-25% reduction from perplexity context helping LLM distinguish legitimate unusual behavior from malicious), Detection Rate (target 3-5% improvement from highlighting anomalous transitions), token cost (target 25-35% reduction from Fast Benign path only, conservative approach), percentage routed to Fast Benign, correlation between perplexity_ratio and ground truth labels, manual review of 5 high-perplexity benign packages to validate that LLM correctly interprets perplexity context.

Name: asymmetric_burden_of_proof
Title: Asymmetric Burden of Proof: Risk-Adaptive Innocence Verification for False Positive Reduction
Experiment: Implement `src/risk_scorer.py` that processes syscalls.log after Step 2. Detect specific suspicious behaviors and compute risk_score: (1) Network operations - detect connect/sendto/socket syscalls, award 15 points, record line numbers, (2) Code execution - detect execve/execveat syscalls, award 15 points, record line numbers, (3) Privilege operations - detect setuid/setgid syscalls, award 20 points, record line numbers, (4) Sensitive file access - detect reads from {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*}, award 25 points, record line numbers, (5) Persistence writes - detect writes to {~/.bashrc, /etc/rc*, ~/.config/autostart/*}, award 25 points, record line numbers. Store detected behaviors as list of {type: str, line_numbers: list[int]}. Implement `src/asymmetric_analyzer.py` to replace Step 4 and Step 6 with three-tier routing: (1) Fast Benign (score < 25 AND zero detected behaviors): label Benign immediately, skip LLM, (2) Low-Risk Analysis (25 <= score < 50): standard analysis prompt with structured output {verdict, confidence, reasoning}, skip debate, (3) High-Risk Innocence Proof (score >= 50): inject explicit behavior list into prompt: 'Suspicious behaviors detected: [enumerate each behavior with type and line numbers]. For EACH behavior, you must provide a specific legitimate explanation with supporting syscall evidence. If ANY behavior cannot be explained as legitimate, verdict must be Malicious.' Use structured output: {behavior_assessments: list[{behavior_type: str, has_benign_explanation: bool, explanation: str, confidence: int}], final_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', reasoning: str}. Validate that len(behavior_assessments) matches len(detected_behaviors). Accept Benign verdict only if ALL assessments have has_benign_explanation=true AND confidence>=70, otherwise override to Malicious. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to compute risk score with behavior detection and route through asymmetric_analyzer, bypassing multi_agent_extraction. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 30-45% reduction), Detection Rate (target 5-10% improvement), token cost (target 40-55% reduction), distribution across three tiers, per-tier accuracy, percentage of high-risk packages where innocence proof fails.

Name: post_hoc_claim_verification
Title: Post-Hoc Claim Verification: Lightweight Evidence-Based Validation of LLM Verdicts
Experiment: Implement `src/claim_verifier.py` that runs after Step 4 (or Step 6 if multi-agent debate is used). Use simple regex pattern matching to scan the LLM's reasoning text for 8 common threat indicators: (1) network patterns - 'connect', 'network', 'socket', 'send', 'C2', 'exfiltrat', (2) execution patterns - 'execve', 'execute', 'exec', 'run', 'launch', (3) credential patterns - '/etc/passwd', '/etc/shadow', '/.ssh/', 'credential', 'password', (4) privilege patterns - 'setuid', 'setgid', 'privilege', 'escalat', 'root'. Apply negation filtering: skip any pattern match preceded by negation words ('no', 'not', 'without', 'zero', 'never') within 5 words to avoid false positives from phrases like 'no network activity'. For each detected pattern category, verify against syscalls.log using simple checks: network category requires at least one {connect, sendto, socket} syscall, execution category requires at least one {execve, execveat} syscall, credential category requires file access to sensitive paths {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*}, privilege category requires {setuid, setgid} syscall. Compute mismatch_score = count of pattern categories mentioned in reasoning but not verified in syscalls. Implement adaptive correction logic based on confidence: If verdict is Malicious AND ((confidence >= 85% AND mismatch_score >= 2) OR (confidence < 85% AND mismatch_score >= 1)), trigger focused correction with transparent prompt: 'Verification Alert: Your reasoning mentions [list unverified categories] but verification found: [list what was checked and not found, e.g., no connect/sendto/socket syscalls for network claims]. Confirm your verdict is correct or revise (max 2 sentences).' Use simple structured output: {revised_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', revised_confidence: int, brief_reasoning: str}. Accept revised verdict as final. If verdict is Benign OR mismatch threshold not met, skip correction and accept original verdict. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to invoke claim_verifier after Step 4 (or after Step 6 if debate is used) and before recording final verdict. Handle gracefully: if pattern extraction or verification fails on any step, skip correction for that package. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 20-35% reduction from catching reasoning-evidence mismatches), Detection Rate (maintain >=95%), token cost (expect 8-12% overhead from correction on mismatched cases only), percentage requiring correction, verdict flip rate after correction (percentage where verification changes verdict from Malicious to Benign), correlation between mismatch_score and initial verdict incorrectness, effectiveness of negation filtering (manually review 10 cases to validate it prevents false corrections).

Name: pattern_specific_micro_validation
Title: Pattern-Specific Micro-Validation: Focused LLM Reasoning on Detected Attack Patterns
Experiment: Implement `src/pattern_micro_validator.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Define 5 high-confidence attack patterns with precise detection rules: (1) Credential Exfiltration - read from {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*} followed by network syscall {connect, sendto} within 50 syscalls, extract the read syscall + network syscall as evidence, (2) Remote Code Execution - network receive {recvfrom, read on network fd} → file write → execve on same path within 100 syscalls, extract all 3 syscalls as evidence, (3) Backdoor Listener - socket → bind → listen sequence within 30 syscalls, extract all 3 as evidence, (4) Privilege Escalation - {setuid, setgid} → execve within 20 syscalls where execve path NOT in {/bin/*, /sbin/*, /usr/bin/*, /usr/sbin/*}, extract both syscalls as evidence, (5) Persistence Installation - write to autostart paths {~/.bashrc, /etc/rc*, ~/.config/autostart/*}, extract the write syscall as evidence. Use simple regex parsing with graceful error handling to detect patterns and extract evidence syscalls (just the syscalls directly involved, no surrounding context). Apply two-tier routing: (1) Fast Benign (zero patterns detected AND zero critical syscalls {connect, execve, sendto, setuid, setgid} AND total_syscalls<25): label Benign immediately, skip LLM, (2) Micro-Validation (any patterns detected): construct a single prompt listing all detected patterns with their evidence syscalls and line numbers, asking the LLM to validate each pattern independently. Prompt format: 'Detected [N] potential attack patterns. For each pattern, determine if it represents malicious behavior or legitimate system activity. Pattern 1: [name] - Evidence: [syscalls]. Pattern 2: [name] - Evidence: [syscalls]...' Use Pydantic structured output containing a list of validations: {pattern_validations: list[{pattern_name: str, is_malicious: bool, confidence: int, reasoning: str (max 2 sentences)}], overall_verdict: 'Benign'|'Malicious', overall_confidence: int}. Aggregate results: if ANY pattern has is_malicious=true AND confidence≥70, final verdict is Malicious. Otherwise Benign. If pattern detection fails or returns empty but critical syscalls exist, gracefully fall back to standard Step 4 analysis. Modify `src/llm_analyzer.py` to handle micro-validation mode with evidence-only format. Update `evaluate_shield.py` to route through pattern_micro_validator before Step 4, skip Step 6 debate when micro-validation is used. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 25-35% reduction from focused reasoning), Detection Rate (target 5-8% improvement from pattern-specific context), token cost (target 65-80% reduction since evidence is typically 2-5 syscalls per pattern vs 50-200 full log), percentage using Fast Benign path, average evidence size per pattern, distribution of patterns detected across benign vs malicious packages, per-pattern validation accuracy to identify which patterns are most reliable.

Name: rule_llm_disagreement_routing
Title: Rule-LLM Disagreement Routing: Cross-Paradigm Validation for Robust Malware Detection
Experiment: Implement `src/rule_risk_scorer.py` that processes syscalls.log after Step 2 to compute rule_based_risk (0-100) using three components: (1) Critical Syscall Score (0-40 points) - count occurrences of {connect, execve, sendto, setuid, setgid, reads from /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow or /.ssh/* or /root/*, writes to ~/.bashrc or /etc/rc* or ~/.config/autostart/*}, award 5 points per occurrence capped at 40, (2) Attack Sequence Score (0-40 points) - detect 4 sequences: read_sensitive→network (20 pts), network_receive→write→exec (20 pts), setuid→exec (15 pts), socket→bind→listen (15 pts), sum and cap at 40, (3) Behavioral Anomaly Score (0-20 points) - compute syscall_diversity = unique_types/total (award 10 pts if <0.25 indicating repetitive behavior), compute interleaving_rate = category_transitions/total (award 10 pts if >0.65 indicating erratic switching), cap at 20. Sum to get rule_based_risk. After Step 4, extract llm_risk from LLM output: if verdict=Malicious with confidence C, llm_risk=C; if verdict=Benign with confidence C, llm_risk=100-C. Implement `src/disagreement_router.py` that classifies into 4 cases using thresholds (HIGH>=65, LOW<45): (1) Strong Agreement (both HIGH or both LOW) - if both>=65, accept Malicious and skip debate; if both<45, accept Benign and skip debate, (2) LLM-High-Rule-Low (llm_risk>=65 AND rule_risk<45) - potential LLM false positive, inject validation prompt into 2-agent debate: 'Cross-validation alert: LLM assessment is Malicious (confidence=[C]) but rule-based analysis found low risk (score=[R]). Verify: Are the identified threats genuine or misinterpreted legitimate behavior?', (3) Rule-High-LLM-Low (rule_risk>=65 AND llm_risk<45) - potential LLM false negative, inject prompt: 'Cross-validation alert: Rule-based analysis detected high-risk patterns (score=[R]) but LLM assessment is Benign (confidence=[C]). Verify: Did the LLM miss subtle malicious indicators?', (4) Moderate/Mixed (all other cases) - standard 3-agent debate without disagreement context. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to compute rule_risk after Step 2, extract llm_risk after Step 4, classify disagreement, and route to appropriate debate configuration. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 30-45% reduction from catching LLM false positives in case 2), Detection Rate (target 3-5% improvement from catching false negatives in case 3), token cost (target 35-50% reduction from fast-path on Strong Agreement cases), distribution across 4 disagreement types, per-type accuracy to validate routing decisions, percentage of cases where disagreement prompts change verdict.

Name: conditional_entropy_determinism_detection
Title: Conditional Entropy-Based Determinism Detection for Scripted Malware Identification
Experiment: Implement `src/entropy_determinism_detector.py` with two-phase operation: (1) Training Phase - at evaluation start in `evaluate_shield.py`, use first 70% of benign packages as training set (skip if <10 benign packages). For each training package, extract syscall sequence and map each syscall to enriched state: (syscall_type, is_critical) where is_critical=True if syscall is in {connect, execve, sendto, setuid, setgid} OR accesses sensitive paths {/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*} OR writes to autostart paths {~/.bashrc, /etc/rc*, ~/.config/autostart/*}. Build 1st-order transition model: count all state[i]→state[i+1] transitions. Keep top 30 most frequent syscall types, map others to 'OTHER'. Apply Laplace smoothing (add-1) and normalize rows to get P(next_state | current_state). For each current_state in the model, compute conditional entropy H(next|current) = -sum(P(next|current) * log2(P(next|current))). Store the distribution of conditional entropies across all states as baseline_entropy_distribution. (2) Evaluation Phase - for each test package, extract enriched state sequence. Skip if sequence_length<10. For each transition, look up conditional entropy from trained model (use median baseline entropy if state unseen). Compute determinism_score = percentage of transitions with entropy<1.0 bit (range 0-100, high values indicate predictable/scripted behavior). Compute critical_ratio = percentage of states with is_critical=True (range 0-100). Generate summary: 'Entropy Determinism: determinism_score=[X]%, critical_ratio=[Y]%, interpretation: [low/medium/high determinism]'. Implement binary routing: (1) Fast Benign Path (determinism_score<40 AND critical_ratio<15 AND total_syscalls<30): varied behavior with few critical operations → label Benign, skip LLM, (2) LLM Analysis with Determinism Context (all other cases): inject concise summary into Step 4 prompt only if determinism_score>=60: 'Determinism Analysis: [X]% of syscall transitions are highly predictable (entropy<1 bit), suggesting scripted/automated behavior. Combined with [Y]% critical operations, this pattern is consistent with automated malware.' Proceed with standard Step 4 and Step 6. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to train model before evaluation loop and route through entropy_determinism_detector before Step 4. Measure on test set: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 20-30% reduction from identifying scripted attacks), Detection Rate (target 5-8% improvement on automated malware), token cost (target 25-35% reduction from Fast Benign path), correlation between determinism_score and ground truth, distribution of determinism_score across benign vs malicious packages (expect malicious to cluster at high determinism), percentage routed to Fast Benign path.

Name: security_state_machine_tracking
Title: Security State Machine Tracking: Detecting Malicious Behavior Through Cumulative State Transition Analysis
Experiment: Implement `src/security_state_tracker.py` that processes syscalls.log between Step 2 and Step 4. Define 5 cumulative security state flags (package can be in multiple states simultaneously): INIT (initial, all packages start here), FILE_OPS (performed file operations on standard paths), SENSITIVE_OPS (accessed /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /.ssh/*, /root/*), NETWORK_OPS (performed connect/sendto/socket syscalls), EXEC_OPS (performed execve/execveat syscalls). Track when each state flag is first set (syscall line number). Additionally track PRIVILEGE_FLAG if setuid/setgid detected or writes to autostart paths {~/.bashrc, /etc/rc*, ~/.config/autostart/*}. Detect 3 unambiguous high-risk state transition sequences: (1) SENSITIVE_OPS set, then NETWORK_OPS set within next 50 syscalls (credential exfiltration pattern), (2) NETWORK_OPS set, then EXEC_OPS set within next 100 syscalls (remote code execution pattern), (3) PRIVILEGE_FLAG set at any point (privilege escalation or persistence). Compute transition_risk_count (0-3) as count of detected patterns. Generate concise summary: 'State Flags: [list active states with line numbers], High-Risk Transitions: [count] detected [list pattern types]'. Apply conservative three-tier routing: (1) Fast Benign (only INIT and/or FILE_OPS flags set AND zero PRIVILEGE_FLAG AND total_syscalls<25): package performed only basic file operations → label Benign, skip LLM, (2) Standard Analysis (1-2 state flags set, zero high-risk transitions): inject brief state summary into Step 4 prompt, skip Step 6 debate, (3) High-Risk Analysis (transition_risk_count>=1 OR PRIVILEGE_FLAG set OR 3+ state flags set): inject detailed summary into Step 4 prompt emphasizing state progression, proceed with Step 6 debate. Modify `evaluate_shield.py` to route through security_state_tracker before Step 4. Measure: Accuracy, F1, FPR (target 25-35% reduction), Detection Rate (target 5-8% improvement on multi-stage attacks), token cost (target 30-40% reduction), distribution across tiers, per-transition-pattern accuracy.
